completed the 12 days of Christmas last Tuesday on January 6th, which marked the actual<|17.32|> <|17.32|> feast of the Epiphany.<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Feast <|6.38|> The problem, of course, is when we are subjected to these kinds of evaluative speech that views<|13.82|> <|13.82|> us more as an object rather than as a subject, there is something in us that begins to erode.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Word by word, sentence by sentence,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> one dismissive look, one critical look,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> one contemptuous look at a time,<|12.02|> <|12.02|> something in us begins to erode.<|16.02|> <|16.02|> And the readings appointed for today insist<|19.02|> <|19.02|> that this is not a small matter.<|22.02|> <|22.02|> At the very heart of the Christian faith<|25.02|> <|25.02|> is the claim that God addresses human beings.<|29.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that being addressed by God is the very foundation of our dignity.<|6.58|> <|7.74|> In the reading from Isaiah today, the passage begins not with action, but with God's address.<|14.86|> <|16.08|> Here is my servant whom I uphold, my chosen in whom my soul delights.<|22.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Before the servant does anything, before the servant's obedience, before his suffering, before his mission, God speaks.<|11.30|> <|13.40|> In the passage from Isaiah, we see that identity precedes achievement and calling precedes accomplishment.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that language is strikingly personal.<|3.10|> <|4.24|> God delights.<|5.20|> <|5.80|> God upholds.<|6.76|> <|7.20|> God chooses.<|7.96|> <|9.98|> The servant's work flows directly from the way that God addresses him.<|15.54|> <|16.82|> A bruised reed he will not break.<|18.66|> <|19.44|> And a dimly burning wick he will not quench.<|21.94|> <|21.94|> Amen.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God's way of acting in the world mirrors God's way of speaking.<|6.50|> <|6.50|> Gentle without being weak, attentive to what is fragile, and refusing to destroy what still can live.<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Psalm 29 celebrates the voice of the Lord as majestic, powerful,<|6.10|> <|7.00|> capable of shaking the wilderness and splintering the cedars of Lebanon.<|10.58|> <|11.78|> And yet the psalm ends quietly.<|14.20|> <|15.66|> The Lord will bless his people with peace.<|18.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God's voice is strong, but it is not the voice of domination.<|5.40|> <|6.96|> God's voice does not overwhelm for the sake of control.<|10.62|> <|11.98|> God's voice orders, it blesses, and it gives life.<|16.46|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that distinction matters because we live in a world that often confuses volume with authority and that confuses force with truth.<|12.92|> <|14.46|> Scripture refuses that type of confusion.<|18.22|> <|20.12|> God's voice is powerful enough to create, but also restrained enough to bless.<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And all of this comes into focus at Jesus' baptism in the Jordan River.<|6.22|> <|8.24|> Jesus steps into the water, and before he preaches, before he heals, before he teaches or gathers disciples, the heavens open and God speaks.<|19.40|> <|20.44|> This is my son, the beloved, with whom I am well pleased.<|27.94|> <|27.94|> Amen.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is not a private spiritual moment.<|3.54|> <|4.24|> It is a public act of divine address.<|6.78|> <|8.26|> God names Jesus in the presence of the community.<|11.68|> <|13.32|> And notice what God does not say.<|16.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> This is the one who will succeed.<|2.76|> <|3.86|> This is the one who will obey.<|5.44|> <|6.44|> This is the one who will prove himself.<|8.22|> <|10.10|> Instead, God says,<|12.12|> <|12.82|> this is my son, the beloved.<|15.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.04|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At the Jordan, Jesus is addressed as beloved before he is tested, before he is evaluated,<|7.72|> <|8.16|> and before he has to prove anything at all.<|10.76|> <|11.60|> And that order matters because in the life of faith, address comes before assessment.<|18.28|> <|19.90|> God does not begin with our judgment or our performance, but with relationship.<|25.84|> <|25.84|> Okay.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The first word spoken over Jesus is not one of instruction or correction, but it's one<|7.16|> <|7.16|> of delight.<|9.48|> <|9.48|> And that same order holds for us.<|12.90|> <|12.90|> We are named by God before we are measured.<|16.82|> <|16.82|> We are claimed by God before we are corrected.<|20.66|> <|20.66|> And we are addressed as beloved before we are evaluated.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Jesus carries that word of God's belovedness with him into the wilderness, into his ministry, into rejection, and with him into suffering.<|13.84|> <|14.90|> That voice at the Jordan becomes the very ground beneath Jesus' feet when other voices grow loud or others fall silent.<|25.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And what happens at the Jordan does not remain with Jesus alone.<|4.50|> <|5.78|> In Acts, Peter declares that God shows no partiality,<|9.22|> <|10.00|> that the same God who spoke over Jesus now speaks across boundaries and cultures and lives.<|16.00|> <|17.42|> And in baptism, God still speaks.<|20.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Baptism is not primarily something we say to God.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> It is something God says to us through water and word<|10.00|> <|10.00|> in the presence of the church.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> God claims us in baptism, God names us in baptism,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> and God addresses a human life as beloved.<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The prayer book does not say that this dignity must be earned or demonstrated.<|4.98|> <|5.88|> It says that in baptism we are sealed and we are marked and we are claimed as Christ's own.<|12.82|> <|13.74|> Not provisionally, not temporarily, but forever.<|18.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And because baptism is public, it's also communal.<|4.20|> <|5.26|> We are baptized into a people who promise together to respect the dignity of every human being,<|12.40|> <|13.10|> precisely because we have first received our own dignity as God's gift.<|18.92|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this is where the gospel presses close to home.<|4.78|> <|5.86|> If our dignity comes from being addressed by God, not because of any capacity we have,<|13.52|> <|14.04|> but simply by sheer address, then the way we address one another is not incidental.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It lies in the very heart of the nature of our own baptismal covenant.<|4.96|> <|6.60|> One of the things I'm reminded of again and again in listening to people speak about their closest relationships is how rarely love fails all at once.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> More often, it erodes through patterns of speech.<|5.50|> <|5.50|> Patterns of speech that are sharp, cynical, dismissive, critical,<|13.68|> <|13.68|> defensive, contemptuous, or withdrawn.<|18.68|> <|18.68|> Long before people stop caring,<|22.86|> <|22.86|> they stop addressing one another with care.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And when that happens, dignity is the first thing that can feel lost.<|7.40|> <|7.40|> Words are never neutral.<|10.24|> <|10.24|> They confer dignity or they can strip it away.<|15.54|> <|15.54|> And we know what it feels like to be spoken about as a problem to be solved,<|21.08|> <|21.08|> a flaw to be fixed, or a position to be defeated.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we know how different it feels to be addressed as a person, especially in moments of disagreement.<|7.30|> <|9.42|> So Christian speech is not simply about being polite.<|13.18|> <|14.30|> It's about recognizing that the person before us already stands under God's address.<|21.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Think about that.<|2.00|> <|2.00|> If we spoke to our spouses and our children and our friends and our colleagues and our co-workers,<|8.00|> <|8.00|> when we look at them, seeing those are individuals claimed by God who stand under God's divine address,<|15.00|> <|15.00|> how might our orientation and our interaction with them be different?<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And if people remember that about us, how might their orientation and their speech be different towards us?<|8.86|> <|9.22|> And how different might we feel in engaging in conversation with others?<|13.94|> <|16.16|> And so this recognition that Christian speech is at the very heart of what it means to be Christians and to live out our baptismal covenant...<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This should change how we speak.<|2.36|> <|3.36|> That we should tell the truth, but without contempt.<|7.16|> <|8.02|> That we can disagree without demeaning.<|11.26|> <|12.00|> And we listen, not merely to respond,<|14.98|> <|15.42|> but to truly receive what the other person has to say.<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In our culture, which is shaped by outrage and reduction and constant evaluation,<|6.14|> <|7.22|> the church is called to practice a different way of speaking,<|10.34|> <|10.90|> one that reflects the voice that named us as beloved before anything else was said.<|18.28|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At the Jordan, Jesus hears the Father's voice before he enters into the wilderness.<|5.80|> <|6.84|> He carries that word when temptation comes.<|9.82|> <|10.92|> He carries the knowledge of his belovedness when misunderstanding follows and when his obedience becomes costly.<|19.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so should we.<|2.36|> <|3.64|> There will be times when dignity feels fragile,<|6.40|> <|6.76|> when words wound, and when God seems quiet.<|9.98|> <|11.32|> Baptism reminds us that the most important word<|14.18|> <|14.18|> has already been spoken to us.<|16.66|> <|17.60|> You are my beloved.<|19.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as baptized people, we are called to let our words at home and in this church and wherever we engage with the world to echo that voice.<|11.78|> <|12.58|> Not loudly, not violently, but truthfully, respectfully, and with dignity.<|20.22|> <|21.44|> Amen.<|21.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.72|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Today is the first Sunday after the Epiphany, the beginning of a new season in the church<|8.94|>
<|10.28|> And we completed the 12 days of Christmas last Tuesday on January 6th, which marked the actual<|17.32|>
<|17.32|> feast of the Epiphany.<|19.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Feast of the Epiphany commemorates the first time Jesus's divinity was revealed to the world.<|7.60|>
<|8.50|> And it was symbolized by a star appearing in the east in the journey of the three wise men to visit Jesus.<|16.14|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This Sunday, as we continue in the Epiphany season, we read about Jesus's baptism, another event in which Jesus's divinity is revealed publicly to all who are present.<|15.88|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is also a manifestation of the triune God for the very first time.<|6.20|>
<|7.34|> God the Father proclaims Jesus as his son and sends the Holy Spirit via a dove to anoint him as the Christ.<|15.88|>
<|16.96|> The word Christ means the anointed one.<|20.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And God does this just as Jesus is about to embark on his public ministry.<|6.36|>
<|7.78|> And this proclamation of Jesus's divinity by God is addressed both to the crowds and also to Jesus.<|16.90|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And what I want to focus on this morning specifically is about the voice of God and the dignity the voice of God confers upon his hearers and what it means for us to be divinely addressed.<|18.90|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.16|> Most of us spend a great deal of our lives<|4.54|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or simply by silent observation based upon someone's first encounter with us.<|7.00|>
<|7.00|> We are often categorized by our age or race, sex, sexual preference, marital status,<|15.00|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Too often we're viewed as problems to be managed or positions to be defeated.<|8.34|>
<|8.34|> But how often are we truly addressed, spoken to in a way that takes into account our full personhood,<|18.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How often are we truly addressed in which we are beheld in the fullness of our being<|5.64|>
<|5.64|> and spoken to in a way that both simultaneously recognizes our dignity<|12.76|>
<|12.76|> and confers our dignity upon us.<|16.18|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I suspect the answer for most of us is not very often.<|6.38|>
<|6.38|> The problem, of course, is when we are subjected to these kinds of evaluative speech that views<|13.82|>
<|13.82|> us more as an object rather than as a subject, there is something in us that begins to erode.<|23.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Word by word, sentence by sentence,<|5.00|>

<|9.00|> one contemptuous look at a time,<|12.02|>

<|19.02|> that this is not a small matter.<|22.02|>
<|22.02|> At the very heart of the Christian faith<|25.02|>

<|7.74|> In the reading from Isaiah today, the passage begins not with action, but with God's address.<|14.86|>

<|16.82|> A bruised reed he will not break.<|18.66|>
<|19.44|> And a dimly burning wick he will not quench.<|21.94|>

<|6.50|> Gentle without being weak, attentive to what is fragile, and refusing to destroy what still can live.<|18.30|>

<|6.96|> God's voice does not overwhelm for the sake of control.<|10.62|>
<|11.98|> God's voice orders, it blesses, and it gives life.<|16.46|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that distinction matters because we live in a world that often confuses volume with authority and that confuses force with truth.<|12.92|>
<|14.46|> Scripture refuses that type of confusion.<|18.22|>
<|20.12|> God's voice is powerful enough to create, but also restrained enough to bless.<|25.90|>

<|8.24|> Jesus steps into the water, and before he preaches, before he heals, before he teaches or gathers disciples, the heavens open and God speaks.<|19.40|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And when that happens, dignity is the first thing that can feel lost.<|7.40|>
<|7.40|> Words are never neutral.<|10.24|>
<|10.24|> They confer dignity or they can strip it away.<|15.54|>
<|15.54|> And we know what it feels like to be spoken about as a problem to be solved,<|21.08|>
<|21.08|> a flaw to be fixed, or a position to be defeated.<|25.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we know how different it feels to be addressed as a person, especially in moments of disagreement.<|7.30|>
<|9.42|> So Christian speech is not simply about being polite.<|13.18|>
<|14.30|> It's about recognizing that the person before us already stands under God's address.<|21.62|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Think about that.<|2.00|>
<|2.00|> If we spoke to our spouses and our children and our friends and our colleagues and our co-workers,<|8.00|>
<|8.00|> when we look at them, seeing those are individuals claimed by God who stand under God's divine address,<|15.00|>
<|15.00|> how might our orientation and our interaction with them be different?<|21.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And if people remember that about us, how might their orientation and their speech be different towards us?<|8.86|>
<|9.22|> And how different might we feel in engaging in conversation with others?<|13.94|>
<|16.16|> And so this recognition that Christian speech is at the very heart of what it means to be Christians and to live out our baptismal covenant...<|25.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This should change how we speak.<|2.36|>
<|3.36|> That we should tell the truth, but without contempt.<|7.16|>
<|8.02|> That we can disagree without demeaning.<|11.26|>
<|12.00|> And we listen, not merely to respond,<|14.98|>
<|15.42|> but to truly receive what the other person has to say.<|20.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In our culture, which is shaped by outrage and reduction and constant evaluation,<|6.14|>
<|7.22|> the church is called to practice a different way of speaking,<|10.34|>
<|10.90|> one that reflects the voice that named us as beloved before anything else was said.<|18.28|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At the Jordan, Jesus hears the Father's voice before he enters into the wilderness.<|5.80|>
<|6.84|> He carries that word when temptation comes.<|9.82|>
<|10.92|> He carries the knowledge of his belovedness when misunderstanding follows and when his obedience becomes costly.<|19.36|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so should we.<|2.36|>
<|3.64|> There will be times when dignity feels fragile,<|6.40|>
<|6.76|> when words wound, and when God seems quiet.<|9.98|>
<|11.32|> Baptism reminds us that the most important word<|14.18|>
<|14.18|> has already been spoken to us.<|16.66|>
<|17.60|> You are my beloved.<|19.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as baptized people, we are called to let our words at home and in this church and wherever we engage with the world to echo that voice.<|11.78|>
<|12.58|> Not loudly, not violently, but truthfully, respectfully, and with dignity.<|20.22|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.72|><|endoftext|>
